description.en: The Abbasid Caliphate (132-656 AH / 750-1258 CE) was the third caliphate to succeed the Prophet Muḥammad. Founded by Abū al-'Abbās al-Saffāḥ in the wake of the 'Abbāsid Revolution, it ruled from Kūfa, then Baghdād (founded 762), then Sāmarrā' (836-892), and again Baghdād. The Caliphate's effective political power began to decline after the rise of regional dynasties (Sāmānids, Buyids, Saljūqs) and ended with the Mongol sack of Baghdād in 656/1258.

temporal.era_label: Abbasid Caliphate (founding to fall of Baghdād)
temporal.note: Cairo Abbasid line (1261-1517) treated as separate continuation entity.

had_capital.note: Kūfa: provisional administrative seat of the early Abbasid revolution.
Baghdād: founded by al-Manṣūr in 145/762 as Madīnat al-Salām.
Sāmarrā': capital under al-Mu'taṣim through al-Mu'tamid.
Baghdād: re-established as capital under al-Mu'tamid; held until Mongol sack.

rulers.name: Abū al-'Abbās al-Saffāḥ
rulers.note: First Abbasid caliph; 'al-Saffāḥ' ('the Bloodletter') is the regnal title.

derived_from.page_or_locator: Bosworth, New Islamic Dynasties, NID-003, p. 6-7.

record_history.note: Initial canonicalization from Bosworth NID-003 (Hafta 2 pilot ETL).

note: Test fixture for dynasty.schema.json: Abbasid Caliphate as paradigm Phase 0 caliphate record (Bosworth NID-003 anchor, Wikidata reconciled, four capital tenure records, partial rulers list — full list of 37 caliphs deferred to P0.2 prosopography phase).
